data.title: June Jobs Report Is Nail In The Coffin, No July Rate Cut
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier today, the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) reported the monthly jobs report for June. The BLS reported that the labor market created 147,000 jobs in June, more than expected. The unemployment rate unexpectedly fell to 4.1%, putting it 0.2% lower than what economists expected, and even further behind the Fed's projections. The unexpected strengthening of the labor market has made the Federal Reserve’s job harder if it wants to lower rates later this month.

Leading Indicators

Going into the jobs report, leading indicators pointed to a mixed picture regarding the health of the labor market. Earlier in the week, the BLS released the Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey (JOLTS) for the month of May. The JOLTS report showed that the labor market was strengthening, with job openings growing to 7.77 million, the highest level of openings since November 2024.

When comparing job openings to the number of unemployed, the shortage of people in the labor market grew to over half a million, the highest level since January. When shifting to non-seasonally adjusted, the labor market is going in multiple different directions when it comes to the sectors that are hiring. Leisure/hospitality and information are the top two sectors with job openings growth, while construction, manufacturing, and government are seeing sizable declines in openings compared to a year ago.

From a flow perspective, hires fell in the month of May and remained under their pre-pandemic levels. While hiring is quiet, so are the separations from the job market, as they also continue to be below pre-pandemic levels. Even quits, which got a lot of attention during the pandemic, remain quiet. Essentially, the labor market has a broad lowering of flows, which is continuing to benefit its strength.

Leading into this morning’s jobs report, the weekly jobless claims data pointed to a markedly weaker labor market. Initial jobless claims, which are individuals seeking unemployment benefits, steadily rose from 235,000 to 245,000 on a weekly basis but did drop to 233,000 and were revised lower for last week. Continuing jobless claims, which incorporate all unemployed workers seeking benefits, saw a notable increase in June from under 1.9 million to over 1.95 million. Despite the growth in benefit requests, the unemployment rate fell.

Rather Broad Strength in the Labor Market

Several other indicators pointed to a continued strengthening of the labor market in June. The U6 rate, which includes marginally employed workers and those working part-time with the desire for full-time labor, is the broadest measure of unemployment, and it also fell 0.1% to 7.7% in June. While U6 has steadily climbed over the last two years, the measure has not weakened in four months, and the spread between U6 and U3 rates remains stable.

In addition to stable U6 readings, the number of full-time people in the labor market continues to grow. On a year-over-year basis, the number declined throughout 2024 but has been positive for the last seven consecutive months. This detail eases speculation that labor growth is coming from individuals taking on second jobs, versus unemployed people being able to find work.

Finally, wage growth, which represented a pain point during the labor shortage and inflationary era, has now become a strength. Average hourly earnings did rise by a less-than-expected 0.2% in the month of June, but that was in line with what investors should expect for a healthy labor market. On a year-over-year basis, average hourly earnings have increased by 3.7%, which continues to edge lower, but is strong enough not to raise concerns about workers’ wages.

Potential Structural Cracks within the Young and Old

While the labor market continues to be strong, it may not be working for everyone. Over the last several months, the unemployment rate among 20- to 24-year-olds has consistently risen and appears to be holding above 8%. In the two years before the pandemic, this level was consistently under 7%, hinting that there may be a structural employment issue in our economy as it pertains to age.

In addition to younger workers, older workers appear to be changing the labor market by leaving the workforce. The number of adults not in the labor force continues to rise and is now over 103 million for the first time ever. While many of these exits are retirements, the fact that the labor force participation rate among workers over 55 remains well below pre-pandemic levels should be noted. If these workers re-enter the labor market, there’s a good chance unemployment will rise, as it appears the supply and demand forces of the labor market are currently at an equilibrium.

Conclusion

While the Federal Reserve continues to insist that the labor market is going to weaken, and their leading data indicators seem to point to that, the labor market continues to strengthen and move away from the Federal Reserve’s 2025 and 2026 targets. The June jobs report, combined with persistent inflation concerns, should remove any doubt that the Federal Reserve will move on interest rates at its next meeting.

data.title: TUA: The Tao Of T-Bonds
data.description:
The treasury market has had a turbulent year so far, as investors continue to assess how America’s fiscal and monetary policies will shape the US economy in the coming years. For the Simplify Short Term Treasury Futures Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:TUA), these changes have helped boost its price; however, the current state of the bond market also raises some risks for this ETF that are worth considering.

This ETF maintains roughly 5X leverage in 2-year US Treasury futures contracts (while the rest of the fund’s assets are mostly short-term T-bills) to simulate the ICE 7- to 10-year US Treasury bond index. This fund is targeted toward investors who want exposure to the duration of 7-10 year T-bonds, similar to ETFs such as the iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (IEF) – although the latter does so by holding US Treasuries at such maturities and doesn’t use leverage. Due to TUA’s difference in holdings, the fund has a higher duration of 9.67 years compared to the IEF’s seven. This difference plays a role in the reaction of each fund to changes in interest rates: The former has higher sensitivity to such changes, which could pay off in case of a rate cut. However, one factor that could affect the ETF’s performance is the shape of the yield curve.

The Yield Curve and TUA

Due to the high uncertainty in the financial markets regarding trade, fiscal policy, including Trump’s Big Beautiful Bill Act, and the Fed’s policy, the yield curve is significantly twisted.

Specifically, the yield curve appears to be inverted at the front end, so that the two and three-year bond yields are much lower than those of short-term bonds, while longer-term bonds are acting more “normal” as they rise along the curve.

What could explain this state of the yield curve? One factor is the term premium (TP) that provides compensation for the added risk of holding a long-term bond over a short one. According to the Federal Reserve Bank of New York calculations, the term-premium has declined for the 2-year and risen for the 7-10 year bonds; so that, the 7-year T-bond’s TP is around 35 bp while the 2-year’s is -10 bp (yes negative!). This sharp contrast suggests a high demand for the 2-year bond, as investors are flocking to such bonds, anticipating interest rates to come down. The market also signals concerns over the long term due to heightened uncertainty about inflation, likely stemming from Trump’s policies on trade.

Despite the decline in short-term rates, long-term rates haven’t fallen by much. This disconnect began in 2024, as shown in the chart below, when examining the 3-month, 2-year, and 10-year yields.

Looking forward, this disconnect could widen. Take, for example, the Big Beautiful Bill Act, which has recently been voted in the Senate and could become law by the weekend. It is expected to add $3.4 trillion to the US national debt over the next decade, according to the CBO. Such a rise in debt is likely to drive higher long-term T-bonds, even as short-term rates fall. The economic ramifications of this legislation may push up the term premium of LT T-bonds, making such bonds relatively cheaper compared to medium-term bonds, which are more affected by short-term economic outlook and inflation expectations over the next couple of years – these factors will play a significant role in the Fed’s policy.

Following the recent FOMC meeting, the Fed maintained its “wait and see” approach and still penciled in two more 25-bp rate cuts this year, likely in September and another in December; hence, the FOMC will likely start to signal a cut by the next meeting in July. However, they have also raised their projections for the rate outlook in 2026 and 2027, to include only one additional 25bp cut in each. In comparison, the markets are still expecting rates to come down to around 3% by the end of 2026.

These dovish expectations have pushed 2-year bond prices up and could affect TUA’s performance. While rising bond prices should help boost the ETF’s performance, if the prices of 2-year bonds rise significantly more relative to the prices of 7-10 years T-bonds, it could affect the ETF’s return as they roll over the future contracts quarterly. And since TUA has a higher duration relative to other ETFs, such as IEF, it’s more sensitive to interest rate changes, which could backfire if the markets are too dovish relative to the current Fed outlook, potentially leading to an upward course correction. One way a market correction could occur is through the progression of inflation.

Inflation and All That Jazz

While the term premium shows high uncertainty at the long end of the yield curve, inflation expectations have remained anchored for the most part.

So, for now, inflation hasn’t reared its head, based on the hard data, despite the high-end uncertainty over Trump’s trade war that could harm the progress the Fed has made in taming inflation. For the bond market, how inflation will develop could shape its progress, if the trade war, which has been marginalized in recent weeks in the news cycle, turn out to have only a transitory and limited effect on the economy and inflation, that would be good enough to let the Fed continue cutting rates, albeit at a more cautious way than it did in 2024. However, there is also a downside risk in which the Fed cuts more aggressively if the economic data shows signs of weakness – hence the high uncertainty in the bond market.

Bottom Line

The current state of the bond market is messy to say the least, while investing in bonds during times of uncertainty could be a prudent thing, this ETF might have some more complexity that could make investing in such an instrument a bit tricky considering its duration, the state of the current medium term bond market, and the yield curve so, while this investment may offer a higher return if and when the Fed were to start slashing rates again, as the market currently expects, the high uncertainty over how the economy will progress due to rapid changes in fiscal and trade policy could heighten the risk of this ETF not paying off.

data.title: Firmer U.S. Jobs Data Suggests No Fed Rate Cut Before September
data.description:
By James Knightley

Jobs report stronger than expected

The June US jobs report shows nonfarm payrolls rising 147,000 versus the 106,000 consensus with 16,000 of upward revisions to the past two months of data. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate surprisingly fell to 4.1% from 4.2% while the market was solidly backing the view that unemployment would actually tick higher to 4.3%. President Trump has been calling for the Federal Reserve to cut the policy rate 200-300bp immediately and two of his appointees (Chris Waller and Michelle Bowman) had suggested that they could vote for a rate cut as soon as this month's FOMC meeting. But the rest of the FOMC is far more cautious and today’s data indicates there will be no rate cut before the September FOMC meeting, especially with tariffs set to push inflation higher over the next few months.

Change in non-farm payrolls (000s)

Details are less rosy

Admittedly, the details are less positive with private payrolls rising only 74k versus expectations of a 100k gain. Government managed to post a 73k increase as a 47k increase in state government workers and a 33k increase in local government workers more than offset a 7k fall in Federal government. Leisure and hospitality added 20k and private education and healthcare services added 51k. So the story is that these three sectors - government, leisure & hospitality plus private education and healthcare services - have contributed 87% of all the jobs added in the past two-and-a-half years. The traditional sectors we would typically associate with a strong and vibrant economy are not adding jobs. We also note that wage growth was more subdued than expected, rising 0.2% month-on-month, while average hours worked per week fell to 34.2 from 34.3.

87% of all jobs are from just three sectors

Trouble may be coming

Our suspicion is that government, private education & healthcare services, and leisure and hospitality will become much less supportive through the year and potentially even act as a drag on job creation later this year and into 2026. Private healthcare jobs are vulnerable to the healthcare spending cuts that are part of President Trump's One Big Beautiful Bill Act and if consumers do start to become more cautious in their spending, reflecting the steep falls in sentiment, then discretionary spending on eating and drinking in bars and restaurants and other entertainment tends to be the first thing that gets cut. We also expect to see Federal government workers shrinking as a legacy of Elon Musk's efforts to get a grip on government spending.

Moreover, our sense that while hiring more broadly has been slowing as the economy loses some economic momentum, we are unlikely to see any meaningful job lay-offs is looking a little vulnerable to recent news flow. In particular the pick-up in Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification notices, which requires most medium- and larger-sized employers to provide notification 60 calendar days in advance of planned closings and mass layoffs of employees, suggests that layoffs could rise from late summer onwards. This tallies with the upward momentum in both initial and continuing jobless claims and the fact that the ISM employment numbers for both manufacturing and service sector firms have been in contraction territory in recent months.

Federal Reserve set to cut rates in the fourth quarter

In terms of what this means for the Federal Reserve, most FOMC members are cautious, wanting to see what the tariff impact on inflation will be before acting in response to potential labour market weakness. This was certainly the tone adopted by Fed Chair Jerome Powell at last week’s appearances before Congress where he stated, "we are well positioned to wait to learn more about the likely course of the economy before considering any adjustments to our policy stance."

With the uncertainty over inflation, the Fed would likely need to see clearer evidence of softness in the form of subdued payrolls growth and a rising unemployment rate to trigger an early move. Market pricing for the Fed this year has slipped from 67bp of cuts to 53bp in the wake of today’s report. We are sticking with our view of 50bp of rate cuts in the fourth quarter, with a further 75bp of cuts in 2026.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: Globus Medical: Obtaining My Backing Again
data.description:
Shares of Globus Medical (NYSE:GMED) have taken a beating so far this year following a soft first quarter performance. Some of this did not come completely unexpected, as I urged for some words of caution in February after the company announced it would acquire Nevro.

This came after the company had seen a very strong 2024, including three consecutive guidance hikes, following the integration of NuVasive, which the company acquired before. The announced acquisition of loss-making Nevro earlier this year made investors uncomfortable (again), making me lose conviction in the thesis amidst demanding valuations.

Following a valuation reset post the first quarter earnings report, valuations have improved a great deal, with appeal readily seen at current levels, despite a hiccup in the first quarter and dilution overhang from Nevro.

Musculoskeletal Disorders

Globus Medical is a manufacturer of musculoskeletal implants, which are designed to treat patients suffering from spine and musculoskeletal disorders. The company offers a complete suite of innovative solutions for both open and minimally invasive surgery, as the company has launched some 150 products since its inception in 2003, on average launching 10 products per annum in more recent times.

The company has rapidly grown, both in an organic and inorganic manner, with the company competing against both larger and less-focused peers like Stryker (SYK) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), to name a few.

Just half a billion business in 2015, the company broke the billion sales mark in 2022 and has grown to $2.5 billion here, largely driven by a $3.1 billion transformative deal to acquire NuVasive, essentially doubling the business with lower margin activities.

Sound operating margins around 30% a decade ago have come down to the high-teens in more recent times, largely driven by the acquisition of the lower margin activities of NuVasive. This deal and other growth have allowed sales to five-fold over the past decade, yet it has come with some 40% dilution incurred over the same period of time.

An $80 stock in February 2023 fell to $60 overnight as the company announced the purchase of NuVasive, as the market was skeptical. That move in isolation with the news made me upbeat in the aftermath of that deal, as Globus Medical was unleashed in 2024 after the NuVasive transaction closed in September 2023.

2024 – Delivering

Following the completion of the NuVasive purchase, the company saw real momentum in 2024. At the start of the year, the company guided for 2025 sales to grow by around 6% to $2.66-$2.69 billion, with adjusted earnings seen between $3.40 and $3.50 per share, driven by further synergies from the NuVasive deal combined with organic growth.

Trading around the $80 mark, the company was valued at $11 billion (on an enterprise basis), resulting in a 4.4 times sales multiple (based on the 2024 sales results).

Bigger news arrived in February as the company announced the acquisition of Nevro, causing shares to shed half a billion in value, after falling from $92 to $88 per share upon the announcement. That was a big deal as Nevro was valued at just $250 million, with Globus Medical set to acquire its struggling and much smaller competitor. The company generated $400 million in sales, yet these were loss-making sales, and hence, it would result in dilution to the 2025 earnings numbers.

The company upped the full year sales guidance to $2.80-$2.90 billion, which feels a bit soft with the deal contributing more than half a year in time to the results. Worse, adjusted earnings are seen down to $3.10-$3.40 per share, suggesting twenty cents per share dilution, equal to a $27 million reduction in dollar terms, although that accretion is seen in 2026 and 2027. This makes me hard-pressed to be upbeat, even as shares look cheap at just over 0.5 times sales (while Globus trades over 4 times sales), yet it comes with significant dilution to near-term earnings seen.

Trading around 25 times earnings, amidst a flattish net cash position, following strong share price action in 2024, I was becoming more cautious after decent returns seen already, and valuations getting more demanding.

Plunging

Since the cautious take in February, when shares were still trading in the eighties, shares have lost quite some ground, driven by a bad news report in May, which reset expectations with shares falling to the $55 mark at the time, now stabilizing around the $60 mark.

In February, the business reported 2024 sales at $2.52 billion, with spectacular sales growth reported at 60% thanks to the full-year contribution of NuVasive, with adjusted earnings reported up seventy-two cents to $3.04 per share. The company reaffirmed the full-year guidance for 2025, both in terms of sales and earnings, pro forma the Nevro deal.

Early in April, the company already announced the closing of the deal with Nevro, with first-quarter sales reported early in May. These were soft, down 1.4% to $598 million, with constant currency sales down 0.8%. This softness was attributed to softer closures, supply chain disruptions, and timing of orders, among others. Adjusted earnings of $0.68 per share were up five cents on the year before. While the company maintained the sales guidance, the adjusted earnings guidance was cut by another ten cents to $3.00-$3.30 per share. Net cash balances of $460 million, excluding lease liabilities, are sustained ahead of the $250 million deal for Nevro, which closed days after the quarter.

Pro forma net cash of more than $200 million is equal to about one and a half dollars per share, based on a share count of 140 million shares. The company is taking advantage of its financial position as the company announced a $500 million buyback program in May. Upon immediate execution, that results in a net debt load of $300 million, sufficient to buy back about 8-9 million shares at prevailing prices, equal to just around 6% of the share base.

What Now?

With 140 million shares trading at $60, the company commands a current $8.4 billion equity valuation and $8.2 billion enterprise valuation, pro forma the Nevro deal. With pro forma sales over $2.8 billion, sales multiples come in below 3 times, and even if adjusted earnings are seen down a bit further, they are still up compared to 2024. At current levels shares trade at about an 18 times adjusted earnings multiple, with this business having real potential to deliver on synergies, not just from the NuVasive deal, but certainly Nevro down the road as well.

Safe to say, that the organic growth is a bit softer, amidst the decline in the first quarter, although it is explained by some items. Following this 25-30% pullback from February levels, I am getting more upbeat again,

Following substantial share price declines, shares are back to levels seen in recent years, in fact, already seen for the first time back in 2018. Current multiples have come in a great deal, reflected both in lower sales multiples and earnings multiples, as the business operates with a rock-solid balance sheet, including net cash balances.

The pullback in the share price coincided with the purchase of Nevro and the soft first quarter, somewhat repeating the negative reaction in the share price of Globus when it announced the purchase of NuVasive. All this makes me more upbeat here, and while the first quarter undoubtedly was soft, it in part has to do with real incidentals, as it is too early to throw the baby out with the bathwater, but it feels too easy to blame softness solely on external factors as well.

Nonetheless, overall valuations look interesting enough to get involved here, as frankly, I am willing to get involved again with the shares, with fundamental support provided at current levels, despite a softer quarter.

data.title: Why Dell Is The Undervalued AI Giant You Can't Afford To Ignore
data.description:
In my last coverage of Dell Technologies Inc. (NYSE:DELL), I rated the stock as a buy based on its strategic shift toward the Infrastructure Solutions Group (ISG) segment and the strengthening momentum of its Client Solutions Group (CSG) segment. Since then, the stock had a moderately positive performance rising 6.33%. However, investors remain cautious in light of recent announcements from top management.

The article below highlights the most important company news, as well as my expectations for the upcoming Q2 FY2026 earnings report.

Quick Update

Let’s begin with the most significant recent news for the company: Dell CEO, Michael Dell, has sold 10M shares and now holds 27.29M shares in the company. Investors may see this move as more cautious due to being an inside sell. While some investors may interpret this insider sale with caution, not necessarily reflect a negative indicator. In my view, this sell is probably a strategic reallocation of Michael Dell’s investment portfolio rather than a signal of declining confidence about the company’s future.

The good news includes Evercore’s bullish position on Dell, as the firm recently raised its price target to $150 from $140. Specifically, the brokerage firm estimates that Dell’s revenue from AI servers will exceed the already ambitious $15B projection. Also, Dell’s storage services are expected to further boost its overall growth due to the expansion of storage margin, according to analyst Michael Fisher.

Furthermore -and not so obvious-is that Dell becomes more attractive to investors through its projections to increase dividends, with the company projecting a 10% annual growth rate.

ISG and CSG Performance

As mentioned in my previous article on Dell, I believe the company’s strategic decision to shift its business model toward the ISG segment is a key driver for its performance.

As the table below indicates, the company has demonstrated consistent yoy revenue operating income growth from its ISG segment over the past five quarters. Additionally, in its Q1’26 presentation, Dell reported an AI-optimized servers backlog of about $14B and total orders of $12.1 billion and has orders of $12.1B – a YoY increase of 365% compared to $2.6B in f Q1’25.

Last about the ISG segment is that the company projected over 50% growth in AI-server shipments from FY25 to FY26, reinforcing Dell’s position as a key player in the AI infrastructure industry.

As for the CSG segment, I highlighted in my last coverage its strong positive momentum. In Q1’26, Dell reported 5% yoy revenue growth in this segment, but also reported a 16% decline in operating income, indicating possibly some margin pressure due to high competition.

However, the company is the leader in commercial AI PC and has increased its commercial PC market share by 6% over the past decade. This is a signal that Dell is well established to benefit from the upcoming end of support for Windows 10 on October 14, 2025, as mentioned in my last coverage.

Both the ISG and CSG segments demonstrate a strong track record, with even stronger potential for future growth. I still believe the company has significant room to grow and the full benefits of its strategic shift toward the ISG segment are not fully reflected in its currently reported results.

Valuation Metrics and My Expectations

Consider the nature of the industry it is better to focus on specific key valuation metrics and do a comparison with its main competitors. According to the company, its main competitors are Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Hewlett Packard Enterprise Company (HPE), NetApp, Inc. (NTAP), Super Micro Computer, Inc. (SMCI), Pure Storage, Inc., and Apple Inc. (AAPL).

Starting with trailing P/E and FW P/E ratios, Dell appears undervalued compared to its competitors -except for NTAP, as shown in the above table. Additionally, both fwd and ttm PEG ratios suggest that Dell is the most undervalued company among its peers, driven by the rising expectations for revenue growth. Also, the EV/EBITDA supports the undervaluation signals indicated by the previous metrics.

Before presenting my thesis, I would like to mention that analyst expects DELL to achieve higher growth than the sector median, as the below table indicates.

However, I don’t fully agree with the above fwd estimations. My main concern is that these estimations don’t reflect the company’s potential, particularly in the ISG segment. The company expects 8% revenue growth for FY26 and 16% growth for Q2’26. I believe these company estimates are reliable, and I give possibilities to Dell to exceed these projections.

The table above reflects my estimation for Q2’26. I project YoY revenue growth of 16%, which is in line with the company’s guidance and EPS to reach $1.64.

Risk Analysis

Geopolitical risks could have a significant impact on Dell’s revenue and growth because Dell operates in various regions. These risks include aspects that the company cannot always predict or control effectively. Although, the ‘tariff war’ between China and the U.S. seems to be coming to an end, still is a crucial aspect that could affect the company.

Also, Dell operates in a very competitive industry with many large players such as MSFT and AMZN. As a result, DELL may face pricing and margin pressure -as above-mentioned - which would lead to slower revenue growth and profitability.

Conclusion

In conclusion, Dell still is an undervalued stock with impressive revenue growth, mainly driven by its ISG segment. Also, its CSG segment leads the commercial AI PC market and the company is well-positioned to benefit from the cyclicality nature of this market.

Overall, its current share price looks undervalued. While analysts expect moderate revenue growth, in my view these estimates don’t fully reflect the company’s potential. Therefore, I remain bullish, and is a buy option for me.

data.title: NYLI Epoch U.S. Equity Yield Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

U.S. equity markets were volatile again during the first quarter of the year, ending the period with mixed returns. Value indices were positive and outperformed growth indices which logged negative returns as the Magnificent 7* collectively fell into bear market territory. Most sectors were positive overall with energy, communication services, and health care generating the strongest returns. Information technology, consumer discretionary, and industrials were the sectors in negative territory.

After a strong start to the year with some indices hitting record highs in mid-late February, stocks ended the quarter on a weak note driven in large part by growth fears that followed uncertainty around tariffs and the broader Trump 2.0 policy agenda. Tariffs were one of the biggest stories in the quarter as ramped up tariff threats in March, declaration of "Liberation Day and an announcement of 25% tariffs on auto imports all weighed on investor sentiment. Softer data, worries over disinflation traction, and cracks in the Artificial Intelligence (AI) secular growth narrative were also among the catalysts that contributed to the selloff in the back half of the quarter. The quarter also featured some increasingly hawkish fedspeak although the Fed's latest Summary of Economic Projections continues to show expectations for two rate cuts this year. As expected, the Fed kept interest rates on hold at 4.25-4.50% during the quarter.

FUND INFORMATION (As of 03/31/25)

Fund Inception

12/3/08

Total Net Assets (all classes)

$1.0 B

Distribution Frequency

Quarterly

Number of Holdings

96

Annual Turnover Rate3 (%)

13

FUND EXPENSES (%)

Class

Total Annual

Fund Operating Expenses

Net (After Waivers/Reimbursements)

A5

1.05

1.05

I

0.80

0.73

Portfolio Highlights

For the quarter, the NYLI Epoch U.S. Equity Yield Fund (the “Fund”) posted a return of 2.1% and slightly underperformed the Russell 1000 Value Index which returned 2.1%. The Fund captured significant upside when markets rose through the first half of the quarter and provided limited downside participation during the sharp decline in March.

Absolute return was positive in most sectors, with the largest contributions coming from health care and utilities. Health care return came primarily from strength in CVS and AbbVie. Both companies were boosted by strong earnings results and bullish guidance from management. Electric utilities contributed most notably to the return in utilities as the sector tends to be a safe haven in periods of volatility.

On a relative basis, the Fund slightly outperformed the broad market benchmark and finished behind the MSCI USA High Dividend Yield Index. Utilities were the largest contributor to relative return, owing to a mix of an overweight allocation to the sector and stock selection. Exposure to select electric utilities was a tailwind to performance. Conversely, the largest detraction came from information technology due to an overweight allocation and stock selection. Performance was hindered primarily by exposure to a few technology hardware stocks that were challenged during the quarter.

What Helped:

Among the largest individual contributors to return were CVS (CVS) and Philip Morris (PM). CVS is an integrated health care services company that operates a nation-wide chain of retail pharmacies, is the largest U.S. pharmacy benefit manager, and provides health insurance through its subsidiary Aetna. CVS's share price outperformed this quarter on the back of a strong earnings report where CVS posted strong results and a guidance that brackets consensus but looks achievable and a good set-up for beating and raising. On the earnings call, management was very bullish and highlighted making "material progress" in stabilizing Aetna's operation and bringing financial discipline to the business. The new CEO also stated that he is "very, very confident and very, very bullish on the continued recovery of that business." Management commentary provided investors with some relief and boosted optimism, sending the share price higher. CVS offers an attractive dividend yield that is well covered by free cash flow, and management remains committed to its capital allocation policy. Philip Morris is a U.S.-domiciled international tobacco company and the manufacturer of the largest international brand – Marlboro. Shares outperformed over the quarter which reflected a positive reaction to the company's fourth quarter results and commentary at subsequent industry conferences. More recently, investors have reacted favorably to weakness in the U.S. dollar (PM reports in USD but earns >85% of revenue in other currencies) as well as its profile as a safe haven in the face of macroeconomic concerns. Longer-term, Philip Morris consistently generates attractive levels of free cash flow and returns cash to shareholders through a significant and growing dividend.

What Hurt:

Among the largest detractors were Broadcom (AVGO) and NetApp (NTAP). Broadcom is a designer and manufacturer of digital and analog semiconductors focused on connectivity. It also develops and maintains software for mainframe applications. Shares underperformed as investors pulled back from AI themed names on worries of a capex slowdown. Early indications are spending on AI will continue to expand in 2026 despite DeepSeek claims of similar results at a lower cost. Revenues have yet to ramp-up and will need to for long term investment but near term the promise of AI is large, both in the transformation of industries and potential productivity gains. Being late or not committing to AI could have drastic consequence which should keep investment elevated over the near term. Broadcom returns cash to shareholders via an attractive dividend with a target of paying out 50% of free cash flow. The balance of cash generation is used to fund debt reduction, share repurchases, and/or accretive Mergers & Acquisitions (M&A). NetApp is a provider of network storage solutions for enterprises. This includes solutions for On-premises, Cloud, and Hybrid solutions. Shares were pressured by poor FY3Q25 results and lowered forward guidance. The company posted lower than expected close rates and pulled back expectations on U.S. government spending given current Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) initiatives. The close rate issue has been addressed with greater oversight. Government spending should resume at the federal level as agencies gain greater clarity on the new administrations' priorities as investment is needed to modernize systems. The company returns ~60-70% of its free cash flow back to shareholders, split between a dividend and share repurchases. The balance of cash generation is targeted toward tuck-in acquisitions.

*The Magnificent 7 stocks are a group of mega-cap stocks that drive the market’s performance due to their heavy weighting in major stock indexes such as the Standard & Poor’s 500 and the Nasdaq 100. It includes Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Meta Platforms (META), Microsoft (MSFT), NVIDIA (NVDA), and Tesla (TSLA).

Outlook and Positioning

  • While the U.S. economy has remained fundamentally strong, stickier than expected inflation alongside turbulence derived from rapid tariff announcements out of the White House have called into question soft-landing expectations. We expect some degree of inflationary pressures, demand erosion, impediments to economic growth and continued volatility as impacts resulting from the recently announced tariffs and a possible prolonged trade war. The ultimate impact on the real economy and corporate fundamentals is largely dependent on the duration and magnitude of tariffs globally. The Fed is in easing mode, but the path forward for monetary policy has been clouded by growth pressures and upside risks to inflation stemming from tariffs. Going forward, supportive policy for the labor market will need to be carefully balanced against the risk of reigniting inflation. Markets' initial exuberance over Donald Trump's victory has reversed, as the aggressive implementation of broad- based tariffs has eroded risk appetites recently. The administration has chosen to lean on tariffs as bargaining chips in geopolitical negotiations, making it difficult to discern what policy proposals are genuine versus what is being used to posture for negotiating power. This uncertainty has driven skittishness in equities and clouded capital market forecasts.
  • Outside the U.S., the geopolitical and macroeconomic backdrop remains more challenging. A general uptick in geopolitical tension has remained an ongoing trend, with broad deglobalization and realignment of global supply chains via onshoring/reshoring and announced tariffs threatening to suppress long term growth potential.
  • We are cautiously optimistic on the path forward for the economy and for equities from a fundamental standpoint, but recent developments place more emphasis on "caution" than "optimism". With the way forward so clouded by the current trade climate, the wide array of possible outcomes is something that investors should be considering while managing risk exposures and positioning for the year ahead. We believe shareholder yield is poised to capture the productivity of a growing economy while remaining defensively positioned for resiliency should growth begin to falter. The Fund offers a diversified portfolio of high-quality companies with reliable free cash flow growth and long-standing track records for sound capital allocation practices, providing durable stability and long-term consistency. History has shown that remaining disciplined has rewarded patient investors over time. No one can predict how or when the current tariff war will be resolved. But we do have a firm conviction that the types of companies the Fund invest in should have the ability to adapt and endure through this environment and even emerge stronger on the other side. At the portfolio level, diversification across regions, sectors, and business models provides an additional cushion against today's uncertainty and volatility.

The opinions expressed are those of Epoch Investment Partners, Inc. as of the date of this report and are subject to change. There is no guarantee that any forecast made will come to pass. This material does not constitute investment advice and is not intended as an endorsement of any specific investment.

Before considering an investment in the Fund, you should understand that you could lose money.

The principal risk of investing in value stocks is that the price of the security may not approach its anticipated value. Investing in smaller companies involves special risks, including higher volatility and lower liquidity. Investing in mid-cap stocks may carry more risk than investing in stocks of larger, more well-established companies. Foreign securities are subject to interest rate, currency exchange rate, economic, and political risks. These risks may be greater for emerging markets. Certain environmental, social, and governance ("ESG") criteria may be considered when evaluating an investment opportunity. This may result in the Fund having exposure to securities or sectors that are significantly different than the composition of the Fund’s benchmark and performing differently than other funds and strategies in its peer group that do not take into account ESG criteria.

1. The Russell 1000® Value Index measures the performance of the large-cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 1000® Index companies with lower price-to-book ratios and lower expected growth values. Index results assume the reinvestment of all capital gain and dividend distributions. An investment cannot be made directly into an index. The Russell 1000® Value Index is generally representative of the market sectors or types of investments in which the Fund invests. 2. The Morningstar Rating for funds, or “star rating”, is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed- end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange- traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar Risk-Adjusted Return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance (this does not include the effects of sales charges, loads, and redemption fees). The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its three-, five-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% three-year rating for 36-59 months of total returns, 60% five-year rating/40% three-year rating for 60-119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% five-year rating/20% three-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10- year period, the most recent three-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods. As of 03/31/25, NYLI Epoch U.S. Equity Yield Fund Class I shares rated four stars (four A) for the overall, five (four A), three (three A) and four stars (four A) in three-, five- and ten-year periods from among 1092, 1030 and 808 Large Value funds, respectively. 3. The Annual Turnover Rate measures how quickly securities in the Fund are either bought or sold during the 12 months as of the most recent annual shareholder report. 4. Average annual total returns include the change in share price and reinvestment of dividends and capital gain distributions. data.title: Kohl's: Demand Remains Poor, And There Is Elevated Credit Risk
data.description:
Investment Summary

My previous investment thought on Kohl’s Corp. (NYSE:KSS) was a sell rating because I expect the macro situation to get worse, and combining that with an elevated inventory position, near-term earnings are likely to see heavy pressure. Fast-forward to today, net sales remain in decline, inventory levels remain elevated despite falling sales, and the once-promising Sephora growth engine has slowed sharply. Additionally, refinancing its debt at a steep 10% interest rate signals the credit market’s low confidence in KSS’s balance sheet. Given these persistent structural challenges and rising credit risk, I maintain my sell rating on KSS.

The Sales Trend Remains Poor

Before I get to the other problems I still see in KSS's equity story, I'd like to address the bullish narrative that KSS's headline sales beat in 1Q25 was a positive sign of turnaround. I don’t think it is. Net sales still fell by 4.1% y/y in 1Q25, with comparable sales down 3.9% y/y, which is about as clear as day that there is still a broad-based weakness. Even the digital channel saw sales fall 7.7% y/y. Management’s decision to merely reiterate its FY25 guidance for a 5-7% sales decline, despite the 1Q25 beat, also suggests that they expect demand to be weaker than they originally expected.

Inventory Overhang Problem

Inventory management was a key concern in my previous bearish note, and this problem has persisted. KSS ended 1Q25 with inventory up 2% y/y, despite a 4.1% y/y sales decline, and while this absolute gap of ~6% is smaller than 4Q24’s ~12%, the y/y delta is still >10% (~6% minus -6.8% in 1Q24). As I said before, this persistent sales-to-inventory growth gap sets the stage for future margin pressure.

Although management said that this was due to pulling forward receipts ahead of tariffs and holding seasonal goods, I am not sure if I should be encouraged by this. Yes, this will likely shield some potential cost increase from tariffs, but remember, this product has to sell. This means this pull forward is effectively a bet on a sales rebound. Looking at the recent performance, it doesn’t seem to me that demand is set for a rebound at all. In my view, this pull forward in inventory simply increases the likelihood of KSS needing to be even more aggressive with promotions and markdowns later in the year to clear excess stock.

Sephora Slowdown Dampens the Hope for a Turnaround

KSS turnaround really depends on two things: stabilizing the core and driving growth with Sephora. I point readers back to the 1Q25 results again, as they show that both these drivers are not delivering. Management explicitly said that it is core that Kohl’s card customer continues to underperform, mainly due to the wrong decision to pull back from categories such as jewelry and petites. While KSS is attempting to win back these customers, I don’t think it is as easy as it seems since regaining lost customers is far harder and costlier than retaining them.

The really troubling aspect is the sharp slowdown in Sephora. Comparable sales growth for Sephora decelerated to just 1% y/y in 1Q25, which was a steep drop from 13% y/y in 4Q24, and the worst thing here is that management hinted that store maturation was a reason for the slowdown. This is a very concerning thing since it structurally changes the growth story to a more difficult one—from “opening new stores” to “driving comparable sales,” of which the latter is way harder to achieve.

With Sephora’s growth momentum significantly reduced, the path ahead for KSS to deliver meaningful sales recovery has become a lot more difficult. The other worry here is that the market will finally see how the core KSS really is performing (since Sephora was sort of lifting up the entire business performance), which means the market may turn even more skeptical of a turnaround if the core KSS business continues to weaken.

We're going to expect these comps not to be as robust as we have fewer new stores opening. 1Q25 earnings.

High-Cost Debt Refinancing Weakens the Balance Sheet

While management has successfully resolved the July 2025 debt maturity overhang I previously highlighted, the way this was resolved created a new layer of risk. To refinance, KSS issued senior secured notes at a cost of a 10% coupon, secured against key company assets. The fact that KSS had to refinance in such a way shows that the credit market views KSS as a high-risk business, which aligns with the May credit rating downgrade from Moody’s to B2. The end result is a materially higher interest burden that will not only weigh heavily on free cash flow and earnings but also restrict KSS's financial flexibility just when it is most needed to support its turnaround.

Also, while they highlighted an adjusted leverage ratio of 2.9x by using shorter lease assumptions, the more realistic unadjusted leverage stands at 4.7x, which does not paint a positive picture of the balance sheet. Combined with declining sales, potential pressured margins from excess inventory, and now significantly higher interest costs, I think KSS is going to have a really hard time servicing its debt.

Valuation

KSS's valuation multiple has come down as expected, from 22x forward PE to 18.8x forward PE, but I still think the stock is trading at a too-high level. Remember that the stock typically trades within a range of 7x to 13x, and the average is 10x, over the past 10 years. It does not make sense to me that KSS should trade at such a steep premium when it is facing strong demand pressure, let alone the increased credit risk. I will leave it to readers to think how much the correct multiple should be, but even if we assume this de-rates to 13x, the downside is still huge.

Risk

The main upside risk is that 1Q25’s beat may actually mark a real inflection point. Management did mention that they are seeing promising early “green shoots,” such as 10% sales growth in jewelry and high-teens growth in petites, which indicates some form of success in re-engaging the core customer. If these trends continue, KSS could see modest sales gains, and this may lead the market to continue valuing KSS at a premium for longer than expected.

Conclusion

KSS’s equity story remains structurally weak, with continued sales decline, bloated inventory, slowing Sephora momentum, and higher interest burdens. With the shares still trading well above historical averages despite the increased operational and credit risk, I still see a downside if the market resets expectations. My view for KSS is a sell rating.

data.title: LifeStance: First Profit, Expanding Margins, And Hybrid Model Make It A Buy Despite Valuation Concerns
data.description:
Investment Thesis

During my research for this article, I got a closer view of the healthcare services sector and came across LifeStance Health Group, Inc. (NASDAQ:LFST). It is a gradually improving name trading at just below $5. The company is silently turning out to be one of the most interesting under-the-radar growth stories in the healthcare sector. Having reported its first quarterly profit ever, with evident signs of expanding its margin, the company is moving on to a more efficient, scalable platform, having been a high-growth roll-up until now. Even after some recent insider selling, Wall Street analysts are bullish, and big-name institutions are doubling down, seeing what the market might be ignoring. As revenue and EBITDA are expected to increase dramatically through 2027, and with a hybrid care model that is not only new but also affordable, LFST might be at the beginning of a long-term re-rating. The surface valuation story is still pretty mixed, but in the analysis below, we will find out just why this can be sensible and whether there is any chance that LifeStance could actually become a good investment in terms of generating real upside in the future.

Company Overview

LifeStance Health ranks among the biggest providers of outpatient mental health services in the U.S., and it has a robust virtual presence with in-person care. The company was founded back in 2017 and is headquartered in Scottsdale, Arizona. It offers many services, including psychiatric testing and individual and group therapy to patients of all ages. The distinguishing feature of LifeStance is its hybrid care model and approximately 70% of visits are virtual, which provides both flexibility to patients and clinicians.

Mental Health Demand Is Surging and LifeStance Is Well Positioned

The mental health crisis is all around and is no longer a silent issue. Between 2019 and 2023, the proportion of American adults who got some type of mental health care increased by almost 5% to 23.9%. At the same time, Telemental health is becoming a lifeline. In January 2024, 66.3% of all telehealth visits were mental health-related, and by 2025, more than 27% of U.S. mental health visits were virtual. The CDC estimates that one in every five adults in the United States lives with a mental illness and over half of the adults will have a mental illness during their lifetime. Nevertheless, the country continues to face a shortage of therapists and psychiatrists, which is causing long waiting lists and unfair access. This is why LifeStance's hybrid model, which combines tech-enabled virtual care with on-the-ground in-person services offered is filling an actual and increasing gap. It's more than growth and a critical reaction to a public health need.

The company's in-network insurance arrangements also assist in making care affordable and accessible which is uncommon in the field of mental health. LifeStance is not only increasing access but in its efforts to bring together mental and physical health, the company is looking to improve patient outcomes and cut down on the overall cost of care. It is a model that does not feel outdated, can be expanded, and is becoming more and more vital in the contemporary healthcare environment.

Real Progress in Q1 2025

LifeStance had a notable Q1 2025, and honestly, the figures speak for themselves. In Q1, revenue was up 11% year-over-year (YOY) to $333 million. This growth was supported by strong fundamentals as the company increased its clinician base by 10% and the volumes of visits grew by 10% too, surpassing the 2.1 million mark. This is a clear indication that the demand for outpatient mental health services is not trailing.

However, the most notable thing was that LifeStance actually turned a profit. The net income of the company was $0.7 million, which is not a great figure when you consider it in absolute terms, but this is a big deal since this is the first positive net income the company has had since its initial public offering. Just a year ago, they were facing a net loss of $21.1 million. That is a huge turnaround and I believe that it would be an indication of a business that is starting to find its groove. Besides, adjusted EBITDA increased by 25% to $34.6 million and Center Margin by 16% to $109.8 million, which demonstrates solid operating leverage.

That said, the cash flow side remains a work in progress. Free cash flow stood at negative $10.3 million, and the cash flow operations moved slightly into the red. However, with more than $134 million in cash and a bit of manageable long-term debt, they have room to continue to invest.

Now, zooming out a bit, we can see LifeStance is transforming. It has been streamlining its physical presence whilst maintaining more than 7,500 clinicians in 33 states. It is obviously putting all the chips on hybrid care, combining real-life and virtual mental health. It is a wise move considering that the need for flexible mental healthcare is still high. Also, they have system upgrades and a superior clinician onboarding process lined up, so I think they are planting a concrete-scale foundation.

LifeStance Is Starting to Show Meaningful Margin Momentum

The next interesting aspect of the LifeStance growth curve is that its margins are growing at an impressive rate.

If we compare it with 2022, EBITDA was at a concerning -$143.6 million on $859.5 million dollars in revenue. That provided the company with an unhealthy negative EBITDA margin of around -16.7%, an indication of a rapidly expanding platform that has not yet found its financial footing. But jump forward to the year 2024, and we can already anticipate a turnaround as LifeStance reported a positive EBITDA of $39.9 million on revenue of $1.25 billion, which is a little over a 3.2% EBITDA margin.

The situation is more intriguing when we look at the direction the trend is taking. Analysts estimate the EBITDA will reach $236.6 million on revenues of $1.845 billion by the year 2027, with the EBITDA margin at about 12.8%. It is an incredible transformation and a change of almost 1,900 basis points in a span of five years.

To start with, it informs us that LifeStance is its top line profitably. The margin growth is in line with the better operating efficiencies of the company, increasing revenue per visit, and decreasing center costs as a percentage of revenues. It also fits well with the latest quarterly performance, in which we already witnessed a gain in Center Margin and Adjusted EBITDA.

For long-term investors, this margin story provides some real depth to the growth narrative. I believe that it is this type of structural transformation that distinguishes hype and actual value creation.

LifeStance Still Looks Pricey, But There Might Be A Good Reason

Pure valuation-wise, LifeStance remains pricey, there is no sugarcoating that.

The forward P/E ratio of 31.79 times is almost twice the sector median and EV/EBITDA (TTM) is sky-high at 44.94. That is the type of multiple that is normally allocated to high-growth tech, not outpatient care providers. Even forward EV/EBITDA, at 16.45, is higher than peers. So yes, on paper, it is expensive.

However, this is where context matters. Although the conventional profitability indicators continue to appear immature, it can be seen that margins are headed in the right direction, and at a rapid pace. The Price/Sales of the company is 1.48 times trailing and 1.37 times forward, both approximately 50% lower than the sector median. And the Price/Book ratio is only 1.33 times, compared with a sector median of 2.31 times. This indicates that the market is resetting, not valuing LFST based on present earnings, but on growth of margins and scale.

What’s Next for LifeStance Stock

With shares trading at around $5, it appears that the market is gradually warming up to LifeStance again. But the question is how much room is left to run.

To get the feel of what is reasonable, we need to anchor on some fundamentals. The company is expected to record EBITDA of $139 million in 2025 and $236.6 million in 2027. It implies that LifeStance is on a good upward earnings trend, and we are in the early innings of that profitability narrative.

Now, let's move to valuation. The stock is trading at about 16.9 times on a forward EV/EBITDA basis, a premium to the traditional healthcare providers but arguably reasonable depending on whether you perceive the long-term margin expansion and scale potential.

If we use a reasonable multiple of 14 times to 16 times forward EBITDA on 2025 estimates of $139 million, we come up with an enterprise value of about $1.95 billion to $2.22 billion. Back out net debt, around $339 million, you end up with an implied equity value of $1.61 billion to $1.88 billion. That is divided by the present diluted shares of around 391 million, and you have a base-case price target of about $4.10 to $4.80. It is somewhat lower than the current price of the stock, indicating that there may not be much upside in the short term unless the company exceeds its expectations, or the market may begin pricing in 2026 growth early.

With an optimistic view that, I believe, will be the case, LifeStance continues to beat its targets, increasing revenue and profitability at a higher rate than most analysts had expected. When the company meets or exceeds its 2025 EBITDA of $139 million and remains on the path towards over $175 million in 2026, the investors could begin to re-rate the stock. A reasonable forward EV/EBITDA of 17 to 18 times would place LifeStance with an enterprise value of about $2.9 billion. Adjusting for net debt, the stock price may be between $6.50 to $7.25.

Such a bullish outlook rests upon the idea that the market is past short-term earnings and views LifeStance as a high-growth, scalable platform rather than another healthcare provider.

Wall Street Sets the Bar Higher

Wall Street analysts, in the meantime, appear all the more bullish.

Analysts are estimating LifeStance stock to have a 12-month price target of $8.86, which represents an 82% upside to where it is currently trading, making it a strong buy. The upper estimate is at $10 and the lower is at $8. It places it in a very narrow and secure range, which implies that the analysts are not only witnessing stability but are seeing a significant improvement in fundamentals. It also suggests that analysts might already be pricing in the LifeStance 2026-2027 margin expansion narrative even though the wider market is not quite there yet.

Insider Sales Draw Attention, but Institutional Gurus Are Doubling Down

Insider activity at LifeStance Health Group has been characterized mostly by stock sales, and no purchases in the open market have been reported in recent months. More prominent is that Chief Medical Officer Ujjwal Ramtekkar sold more than 25,000 shares in two batches in June 2025, with other executives, such as Chief Digital Officer Pablo Pantaleoni and Director Robert Bessler also having cut positions earlier in the year. Although the lack of insider buying is something to raises the eyebrows, it is worth noting that insider sales are frequently motivated by regular financial planning, diversification, or vesting schedules as opposed to a lack of belief in the future of the company.

Despite the insider selling at LifeStance, institutional sentiment offers us a more positive outlook. Guru positioning has been strong in recent months with several big-time gurus taking positions in or adding to LFST positions including Steven Cohen, Joel Greenblatt, Paul Tudor Jones, and First Eagle Investment. It is worth noting that First Eagle added about two big positions in Q1 2025, and Renaissance Technologies increased its holding by almost 90%. Even Steven Cohen after a small sell-out in late 2024 re-entered in March 2025 with a large purchase. This indicates that experienced investors find long-term value in the stock. Although insider sales are an issue of concern, simultaneous stock build by institutional giants offers a healthy check and perhaps is even indicative of confidence in the overall growth trend of LifeStance.

Risks To My Thesis

Although the long-term expansion story of LifeStance is promising, investors should watch out for some risks. To begin with, the company is not yet consistently free cash flow positive. Although the EBITDA has improved lately, Q1 2025 negative cash flow indicates that operational tightening is yet to occur. The margin expansion story will be slow in case of any glitches in the cost control process particularly in the labor or tech infrastructure costs.

Second, a strong use of virtual care has its regulatory and reimbursement uncertainty, despite being a strength. Telehealth changes in policy might also affect the number of visits and the clinician's efficiency. Thus, LifeStance will have to tread carefully on any of these fronts, or its rerating potential can easily be re-evaluated by the market.

Finally, the general mental health domain is getting crowded, and social media is erasing the difference between actual care and therapy jargon. According to a recent LifeStance survey, almost one-third of Americans have attempted to self-diagnose mental issues using social media posts, which are usually not followed up by a professional. That is a serious issue in the mental health industry as a whole, but it is also a chance. The survey has also revealed that 81% of Americans do not consider social media as an alternative to clinical treatment. That supports the company's positioning as a reliable, evidence-based alternative to a dense environment.

Your Takeaway

LifeStance Health is not just casually establishing a way forward. It has recorded its first-ever net profit and its margins are on a healthy upward trend, so this is not a mere case of throwing growth at the wall and seeing what sticks. Yes, the valuation picture is still cloudy, and cash flow is not yet on board, but that has not deterred big-name institutional investors from leaning in. Take a step back and what you will see is a company that is finally aligning its growth ambitions with some real operational rigor. Combine that with a hybrid-model structure that feels spot-on to the future, enhanced clinician onboarding, and a high cash cushion, and it is clear that LifeStance is creating a decent slice of value in an already crowded industry. Recent insider selling is no doubt noteworthy, however, institutional buying and tightening fundamentals are more definitive. I believe that the market is still underestimating the progress made by this company and where it is headed. For long-term investors, I am positive and assign LifeStance a Buy rating as of now.

data.title: Live Oak Bancshares: Earnings Outlook Is Positive, But Stock Seems Overvalued
data.description:
Earnings of Live Oak Bancshares, Inc. (NYSE:LOB) will likely grow this year due to lower provisioning expense and a higher average loan balance. However, there are significant headwinds ahead because of an anticipated slowdown in the loan growth and a reduction in the net interest margin. My valuation analysis shows that Live Oak Bancshares is currently trading a bit higher than its fair value; hence, I'm adopting a hold rating on the company.

Strong Loan Growth to Lose Steam Due to Macroeconomic Factors

Live Oak Bancshares' loan portfolio grew by a strong 4.4% in the first quarter of 2025, in line with the incredible growth seen over the last two years. The management was optimistic about future loan growth in the conference call, as shown by the following quote:

Our strong PPNR growth continues with excellent loan and deposit production leading to strong revenue growth well in excess of expenses, and I expect this to continue. Key growth initiatives, such as Live Oak Express, which is our small-dollar SBA loan program, and acquiring checking relationships are continuing to ramp.

Despite the management's guidance, I think some slowdown after the recent strong growth is likely because of the macroeconomic outlook. The World Bank, as well as U.S. CFOs surveyed by the Richmond Fed, are expecting the country's GDP to halve to 1.4% in 2025 from 2.8% in 2024. The expectations of an economic slowdown are mostly attributable to the tariffs and counter tariffs.

Further, the slowdown has already started becoming visible on the job market. The latest National Employment Report by ADP shows that private employers shed 33,000 jobs in June. Economists surveyed by Reuters had been expecting a gain of 95,000, which means the job report is quite disappointing. As of the writing of this report, the June jobs report by the Bureau of Labor Statistics has not been released.

Live Oak Bancshares operates nationwide and mostly lends to small businesses. Small business loans make up 64% while commercial banking loans make up the remaining 36% of total loans. The NFIB Small Business Optimism Index improved in May to 98.8, which is slightly higher than the 51-year average of 98.0. Nevertheless, I believe that small businesses will suffer more from tariffs compared to bigger businesses because bigger businesses have more resources. Firstly, small businesses usually have lower working capital, so they might not be able to foot the bill of tariffs upfront. Furthermore, small businesses have lower negotiating power with respect to their foreign suppliers, so most of the tariff burden, if not all, will fall on them. In contrast, big businesses might be able to convince their foreign suppliers to lower their prices, thereby absorbing some of the tariff impact.

Considering these factors, I'm expecting the loan growth to slow down to 2.0% per quarter for the last three quarters of this year. Because of the recent strong growth, the average loan balance for this year will be much higher than the average balance for last year. I'm expecting average loan balance growth to be 16%, and period-end loan balance growth to be 10.7%. The following table shows my period-end estimates for key balance sheet line items.

Margin to Depend on Future Fed Funds Rate Direction

Live Oak Bancshares' net interest margin improved by 5 basis points sequentially in the first quarter of 2025 after declining for the last two years.

However, the first quarter's improvement is not indicative of a turnaround in the downtrend. The management mentioned in the conference call that Live Oak Bancshares' subsidiary, Live Oak Banking Company, is an asset-sensitive bank with both its lending and funding primarily tied to the short end of the curve. Asset-sensitive means that asset re-pricing outweighs liability re-pricing, so in a falling interest-rate environment, loan yields will fall more than deposit costs, leading to a contraction of the net interest margin.

Moreover, as LOB's lending and funding are tied to the short end of the yield curve, they will be sensitive to changes in the Fed funds rate. The short end is more affected by the Fed funds rate than the longer end of the yield curve. (This is an interesting article about the relations between different interest rates, in case you want to understand in greater detail.) Hence, LOB's net interest margin direction is highly dependent on the trajectory of the Fed funds rate.

Like the Federal Open Market Committee's projections, I'm expecting a 50 basis point rate cut later this year. I believe the anticipated economic slowdown this year will prompt rate cuts. The Fed is expecting the GDP growth rate to be slashed to 1.4% this year from 2.8% last year. Further, the Fed's Chair recently remarked that they would've already cut interest rates if it hadn't been for the threat of tariffs raising inflation, as per news reports.

A big risk in this scenario is that tariffs will push up inflation too much, which will discourage the Fed from cutting rates. The survey by the University of Michigan shows that inflation expectation is at 5.0%, while the survey by the New York Fed shows that inflation expectation is at 3.2% for next year. Both surveys indicate that inflation will probably be above the Fed's target of 2.0%. Therefore, there is a chance, albeit small, that the Fed won't cut rates at all this year. According to news reports, the U.S. president is considering naming the next Fed Chair early so that the new chairperson can cut rates, which adds to the uncertainty.

Considering all these moving factors and the uncertainties, I'm expecting the Fed to cut rates by 50 basis points later this year. Consequently, I'm expecting the net interest margin to drop by 9 basis points in the last three quarters of 2025.

Lower Provisioning to Contribute to Earnings Growth

Although I'm expecting the loan growth to slow down, the average loan balance will be high enough to undermine the expected net interest margin decline this year. Additionally, the provisioning expense for expected loan losses will likely decline this year, which will lift the bottom line. I'm expecting the provisioning expense to be lower this year because I'm expecting loan additions to be lower, as I discussed above.

Moreover, I'm expecting the non-interest income and efficiency ratio to be close to last year's level.

Overall, I'm expecting Live Oak Bancshares to report an EPS of $2.01 for 2025, up by 19% year-over-year.

Adopting a Hold Rating

As can be seen in the income statement summary table above, Live Oak Bancshares's earnings have been quite volatile from year-to-year. As a result, the price-to-earnings (P/E) multiple has had a very wide range over the last four years, from below 10x to north of 30x. Therefore, the P/E multiple cannot be relied upon to remain stable in the future.

I think in this case, it's more appropriate to take the peer average P/E and price-to-tangible book (P/TB) multiples to value the company.

LOB HOPE VBTX TCBK RBCAA Peer Average
P/E (TTM) 24.4 14.7 13.4 12.4 12.8 13.3
P/B (TTM) 1.41 0.64 0.91 1.11 1.44 1.0
P/TB (TTM) 1.41 0.82 1.24 1.48 1.50 1.3

Source: Seeking Alpha

Multiplying the average P/E ratio by my EPS forecast gives a target price of $26.7 for the end of 2025.

Multiplying the average P/TB ratio by my TBVPS forecast gives a target price of $31.3 for the end of 2025.

The average price target comes out to $29.0, which suggests an 8.6% downside from the July 2 closing market price.

Live Oak Bancshares has maintained its quarterly dividend at $0.03 per share since the middle of 2017. This dividend level gives a small dividend yield of 0.4%. Adding this dividend yield to the price downside gives a total expected return of negative 8.2%.

I think Live Oak Bancshares is a profitable company whose earnings are set to grow this year. Unfortunately, the stock seems to be trading at a premium to its fair value. I think a negative 8% total expected return doesn't warrant a sell rating. Moreover, earnings are on an uptrend. Hence, I'm adopting a Hold rating on Live Oak Bancshares.

Please note that Seeking Alpha's Quant Model currently has a Strong Sell Rating on Live Oak Bancshares, the reasons for which are explained in this article.

data.title: Rivian: Turnaround Story With R2 Still Needs A Reality Check (Upgrade)
data.description:
Rivian: Transition To R2 Affected Q2's Production

I hope you weren't mistaken about chasing the bump in the stock of Rivian Automotive, Inc. (NASDAQ:RIVN) in May 2025, after buyers helped it make an incredible recovery. I doubled down on my bearish rating, underscoring why the surge in RIVN should prove to be unsustainable. With the stock having underperformed the market since my previous RIVN writeup, is it time for us to consider whether EV upstarts like Rivian can finally find their footing in their turnaround game?

My update comes hot on the heels of Rivian's second quarter deliveries and production update for Q2. As you might already know, the company slowed down its production markedly in the second quarter to prime its production line for the new R2 adjustments. A quick recap:

  • Produced 5,979 vehicles for Q2, but delivered 10,661 vehicles. That marked drop in production shouldn't have surprised anyone because the mid-sized R2 launch retooling has already been anticipated.
  • Despite that, management has maintained its full-year deliveries outlook of between 40,000 and 46,000 vehicles.
  • Holding back production suggests an active management approach to avoid overproducing the R1 line, but also reminds us of the production challenges in between R1 and R2 that we must continue to scrutinize.

Rivian: Tesla's Miserable Deliveries Could Spell Further Challenges

Note that Rivian has already downgraded its outlook back in Q1. So, the company has reassured investors that a forward ramp from FY2026 shouldn't affect the current delivery cadence. Yet, questions could still be asked whether the market for EVs could weaken further following Tesla's (TSLA) Q2 report, which saw its deliveries fall by 13.5%.

Of course, we could argue that Tesla's global numbers have also been stymied by relatively weak performances in Europe and an increasingly competitive Chinese EV market. In contrast, Rivian's primary focus remains on the domestic EV market, looking to enter lower-priced segments, as its premium R1 business appears to be reaching a plateau in deliveries. Why?

Note that Rivian's deliveries for Q2 already marked a sharp decline of more than 22% YoY. And with the company banking on keeping its guidance for the full year intact, RIVN is obliged to deliver almost 24K vehicles in the second half just to make sure it meets the midpoint of its outlook range. I think CEO RJ Scaringe and his team will have several questions awaiting them about how they intend to keep their guidance when the momentum for EV adoption seems to be inundated with multitudinous headwinds coming at them from nearly every facet of the market.

  • Political headwinds that saw Elon Musk and President Trump spar once more over the accelerated phase-out of the EV credits.
  • While interest rates are expected to be reduced four times in the next 12 months, the Fed could be willing to stay the course for an extended period and not acquiesce to the market's expectations if tariff-related inflationary shocks turned out to be more pronounced than anticipated.
  • Due to the relatively weak performance for Tesla in Q2, the company might seek to overcome the pessimistic narrative and set H2 up better by potentially leveraging price cuts to take market share from peers. Already, we saw that occurring in the Chinese EV market recently, as BYD Company (OTCPK:BYDDF) and its peers geared up for an intensely arduous EV price war that also earned the ire of China's regulators.

Rivian: All Eyes On How Rivian Intends To Ramp R2

And with Rivian positioning itself to transition into the mid-sized R2 platform, we might have to contend with several execution challenges. It's increasingly obvious from Rivian's Q2 deliveries/production release that it may even need to consider accelerating its R2 launch to keep pace with the underlying changes in the market.

And recall that Wall Street is banking on Rivian seeing a sharp reacceleration in growth from FY2026 (up 40% in 2026, and then another 56% in 2027). Therefore, it suggests that analysts expect the lower-cost platform ($45K R2) to be capable of somehow usurping market share from Tesla and legacy peers quickly, amidst convoluted underlying EV market challenges right now.

Reasonable? I think it's way too soon for us to ascertain. But, credit must still be given to Rivian for making headway and committing to prepare for R2 quickly, notwithstanding the near-term impact on its production numbers. Yet, with Rivian being starved of scale and clearly unprofitable, I'm not sure whether (even with the timely financial support from Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY)) it's enough to convince investors about its ability to ramp production adequately through 2027.

Buy Or Sell RIVN Stock?

The resistance zone at the $16 level reacted negatively again back in May 2025. Incidentally, that level also corresponds with the selling fervor assessed in December last year. Coincidence?

Rather than thinking that it's so "coincidental", why don't we consider that RIVN sellers had waited for buyers to rush in, before unloading their slew of sell orders to eviscerate the upward momentum.

Despite that, it's also plausible that RIVN buyers have been buying up significant pullbacks, as seen in the three consecutive higher-lows structures since its nadir in early 2024. Hence, the price action does suggest that it may be worthwhile first to consider where the current pullback lands before thinking about picking up the pieces once more.

Rating: Downgrade to Hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
